item.title: iPhone 15 vs iPhone 15 Plus: Which phone should you buy?
item.itunes:title: iPhone 15 vs iPhone 15 Plus: Which phone should you buy?
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
• iPhone 15
    Great entry-level phone

    The iPhone 15 is the base model of the 2023 iPhone lineup. It offers a 6.1-inch screen and the same A16 Bionic chipset found in the iPhone 14 Pro. Plus, it features an IP68 rating and a very capable camera setup.

    Pros
    • Powerful A16 Bionic chipset
    • 48MP main camera
    • Gorgeous 6.1-inch screen
    Cons
    • USB-C limited to USB 2.0 speeds
    • Less battery life than the iPhone 15 Plus
    $800 at Best Buy $799 at Apple
  • Source: Apple
    iPhone 15 Plus
    Larger, more powerful

    The iPhone 15 Plus is the larger base model of the 2023 iPhone lineup. It offers a 6.7-inch screen but the same A16 Bionic chipset and camera setup. It has slightly more battery life than the base model iPhone 15, and it has a larger 6.7-inch screen.

    Pros
    • Powerful A16 Bionic chipset
    • 48MP main camera
    • Larger 6.7-inch display
    Cons
    • USB-C limited to USB 2.0 speeds
    • Slightly bulkier than the iPhone 15
    $900 at Best Buy $899 at Apple

Apple unveiled the iPhone 15 and iPhone 15 Plus at its 2023 event. Much like previous generations, Apple has a base iteration, a Plus variant, and a Pro lineup for its flagship smartphone. The iPhone 15 and iPhone 15 Plus may not be top-of-the-line models, but they still offer plenty to like. In fact, many features found on last year's Pro have now made it to the base lineup, including Dynamic Island support. So, which should you choose?

Price, specs, and availability

The iPhone 15 and iPhone 15 Plus will be available for preorder starting September 15. Both models come in Blue, Pink, Yellow, Green, and Black. The iPhone 15 starts at $799 for the 128GB model and goes up to $1,099 for 512GB. Meanwhile, the iPhone 15 Plus starts at $899 for 128GB and tops off at $1,199 for 512GB. These debut prices are the same as the iPhone 14 and iPhone 14 Plus's launch prices. Additionally, eligible trade-ins can net you up to $800 off. You can buy the iPhone 15 and iPhone 15 Plus directly from Apple, but if you want a version tailored to your cellular provider, that's also an option. They'll also be available at all the usual third-party retailers like Amazon and Best Buy.


  		• iPhone 15iPhone 15 Plus
    SoCApple A16 BionicApple A16 Bionic
    Display6.1-inch OLED Super Retina XDR6.7-inch OLED Super Retina XDR
    Storage128GB, 256GB, 512GB128GB, 256GB, 512GB
    PortsUSB-CUSB-C
    Operating SystemiOS 17iOS 17
    Front camera12MP TrueDepth camera12MP TrueDepth camera
    Rear cameras48MP f/1.6 main, 12MP f/2.4 ultra wide48MP f/1.6 main, 12MP f/2.4 ultra wide
    Dimensions5.81 x 2.82 x 0.31 inches (147.6 x 71.6 x 7.80 mm)6.33 x 3.06 x 0.31 inches (160.9 x 77.8 x 7.80 mm)
    ColorsBlue, Pink, Yellow, Green, BlackBlue, Pink, Yellow, Green, Black
    Weight6.02 ounces (171 g)7.09 ounces (201 g)
    IP RatingIP68IP68
    Price$799$899
    GPU5-core GPU5-core GPU
    ChargingUSB-C (20W), MagSafe, Qi2USB-C (20W), MagSafe, Qi2

Design and build

Source: Apple

Looks are subjective, but there are some objective similarities between the iPhone 15 and iPhone 15 Plus. They actually look very similar, except for their size, weight, and battery capacity. If you want a more compact phone, go for the 6.1-inch iPhone 15, but if you want a larger display, choose the 6.7-inch iPhone 15 Plus. Note the latter is heavier — about 1 ounce — which might make a difference to you. You'll also likely want a great case for either one to protect their glass bodies from scratches and drops, which might add to the overall weight and size.

Source: Apple

Both phones look similar to their forerunners, but Apple claims that all the color variations of this phone use a special process that embeds the color within the glass back. Both models use an aluminum frame. All variations feature a matte finish. On the back are the two cameras, and around the front, you'll see the notch where the Dynamic Island expands from and where the selfie camera resides.

Display

Source: Apple

The display is really where these two phones differ most, and even then, it's not by much. The base model features a 6.1-inch Super Retina XDR display, while the iPhone 15 Plus has a 6.7-inch Super Retina XDR display. Both are OLED and feature True Tone, haptic touch, and max out at 2,000 nits of brightness, which is brighter than the iPhone 14. Both also support the Dynamic Island feature first introduced on the iPhone 14 Pro.

Since both displays are identical in all but size, it comes down to your personal preference. The 6.7-inch display will give you more room for scrolling and opening apps, but the smaller screen will be easier to hold and navigate.

Performance and software

SourceL Apple

Both the iPhone 15 and iPhone 15 Plus will feature iOS 17 and have the same internals, so they should run it similarly. They both feature an A16 Bionic chip — the same one from the iPhone 14 Pro series — which consists of a 6-core CPU, a 5-core GPU, and a 16-core neural engine. That chipset made the iPhone 14 Pro one of the best phones out there, and while it has been superseded with the A17 Pro in the new Pro models, it's still quite powerful. We had no complaints when we tested this chipset last year.

In less technical terms, the iPhone 15 and iPhone 15 Plus both use their chip and machine learning capabilities for processing images, including adjusting focus. New this year is a feature that can turn a regular shot into a portrait after you take it and switch focus between people in a shot.

Again, there's a tie here because the internals are the same. You should not see too many snags or slowdowns with either model.

Source: Apple

Battery capacity is the only other major difference besides screen size for the iPhone 15 and iPhone 15 Plus. The latter boasts a slightly larger battery, and as a result, it's rated to last up to 26 hours when playing video, while the iPhone 15 lasts up to 20. Other than that, however, both phones use USB-C to charge, a welcome departure from Apple's proprietary Lightning connector.

Both phones also feature wireless charging capabilities via MagSafe and Qi, and both allow fast charging using a 20W or higher charger. As expected, you have to buy the adapter separately.

Camera

Another tie, as you've come to expect by now. Both of these phones have the same camera setup. On the back, you'll find a 48MP f/1.6 sensor and an ultrawide 12MP f/2.4 sensor. Both feature Smart HDR 5 and advanced portrait capabilities, which means low-light shots and images of people are likely to turn out great. As mentioned, these phones can use their advanced chipset to help your photos look better, too.

The iPhone 15 and iPhone 15 Plus both use a quad-pixel sensor and have 2x telephoto capabilities. Both can capture shots at 0.5x, 1x, and 2x zoom. You can also capture 4K video at 24, 25, 30, or 60 FPS. The selfie camera takes 12MP shots and also features advanced portrait effects and Smart HDR 5. It can capture 4K video at the same FPS rates as the rear camera as well.

Which is right for you?

After taking a look at all of their features, it's clear that the iPhone 15 and iPhone 15 Plus are pretty evenly matched. The latter is slightly heavier but comes with added battery life and a larger display, but those are the only real differences between them. Respectively, they start at $799 and $899 in the U.S. If you want to save some cash or want a lighter, more compact option and don't mind slightly less battery life, opt for the iPhone 15.

iPhone 15
Smaller and lighter

The iPhone 15 is the base model of the 2023 iPhone lineup. It offers a 6.1-inch screen and the same A16 Bionic chipset found in the iPhone 14 Pro. Plus, it features an IP68 rating and a very capable camera setup.

$800 at Best Buy $799 at Apple

If price is no object, or you want to squeeze out more battery capacity, then go for the iPhone 15 Plus. You get up to 26 hours of continuous video playback and a bigger 6.7-inch screen. Just keep in mind it's slightly bulkier and heavier.

Source: Apple
iPhone 15 Plus
Bigger with more battery life

The iPhone 15 Plus is the larger base model of the 2023 iPhone lineup. It offers a 6.7-inch screen and the same A16 Bionic chipset found in the iPhone 14 Pro. Plus, it features an IP68 rating and a very capable camera setup. It has slightly more battery life than the base model iPhone 15.

$900 at Best Buy $899 at Apple

item.title: Apple may soon start wirelessly updating sealed iPhones before sale
item.itunes:title: Apple may soon start wirelessly updating sealed iPhones before sale
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Apple has developed a pad-like device to install the latest iOS updates on iPhones before they are opened, ensuring a smooth user experience from the start.
  • The device will be kept in Apple retail stores for use by their Specialists, who will update the new iPhones before handing them over to customers.
  • This unique approach by Apple aims to address common issues like overheating and software bugs in newly launched phones by providing fixes before users even open the box.

From mixed-reality headsets to new services, our predictions and rumors about what Apple might launch in 2023 were spot on. However, Apple was quietly working on one more product outside our knowledge, the details of which have only emerged recently. It seemingly has a single purpose: to help you get the latest iOS experience after switching on your iPhone for the first time.

Apple has developed a pad-like device for installing the latest iOS updates to new iPhones without opening their boxes. This will ensure users don't go through the extra step of installing the latest software update after buying an iPhone. It is useful in a situation when you're buying a model a few months or more after its official launch or one that originally shipped with an older version of iOS.

From the details provided by Mark Gurman of Bloomberg, it's evident that the device in question is not meant for sale to consumers. Instead, Apple will keep them in its retail stores to be used by its "Specialists" to upgrade the new iPhone to the latest iOS version before handing it over to users. No, it's not just about providing the users with all the latest and greatest new features.

Issues like overheating and other software-related bugs are quite common in newly launched phones. Phone makers release software updates to fix those issues via software updates, like how Apple fixed the overheating iPhone 15 devices by pushing the iOS 17.0.3 update. The difference Apple's upcoming updater device will make is that those issues will be addressed before users first open the boxes and start using their iPhones, ensuring a smooth and flawless experience right off the bat.

The way it works is pretty simple. Apple will place the boxes of iPhones on top of the updater, which, in turn, will turn on the iPhone wirelessly, update the phone to the latest iOS version, and switch it back off. Apple has plans to begin rolling out the physical iOS updater tool to its retail stores before the end of this year, which is when you'll know what it looks like unless, of course, someone leaks the information or images of the device beforehand.

  • The device will be kept in Apple retail stores for use by their Specialists, who will update the new iPhones before handing them over to customers.
  • This unique approach by Apple aims to address common issues like overheating and software bugs in newly launched phones by providing fixes before users even open the box.

From mixed-reality headsets to new services, our predictions and rumors about what Apple might launch in 2023 were spot on. However, Apple was quietly working on one more product outside our knowledge, the details of which have only emerged recently. It seemingly has a single purpose: to help you get the latest iOS experience after switching on your iPhone for the first time.

Apple has developed a pad-like device for installing the latest iOS updates to new iPhones without opening their boxes. This will ensure users don't go through the extra step of installing the latest software update after buying an iPhone. It is useful in a situation when you're buying a model a few months or more after its official launch or one that originally shipped with an older version of iOS.

From the details provided by Mark Gurman of Bloomberg, it's evident that the device in question is not meant for sale to consumers. Instead, Apple will keep them in its retail stores to be used by its "Specialists" to upgrade the new iPhone to the latest iOS version before handing it over to users. No, it's not just about providing the users with all the latest and greatest new features.

Issues like overheating and other software-related bugs are quite common in newly launched phones. Phone makers release software updates to fix those issues via software updates, like how Apple fixed the overheating iPhone 15 devices by pushing the iOS 17.0.3 update. The difference Apple's upcoming updater device will make is that those issues will be addressed before users first open the boxes and start using their iPhones, ensuring a smooth and flawless experience right off the bat.

The way it works is pretty simple. Apple will place the boxes of iPhones on top of the updater, which, in turn, will turn on the iPhone wirelessly, update the phone to the latest iOS version, and switch it back off. Apple has plans to begin rolling out the physical iOS updater tool to its retail stores before the end of this year, which is when you'll know what it looks like unless, of course, someone leaks the information or images of the device beforehand.

item.title: How to split screen on a Chromebook in clamshell or tablet mode
item.itunes:title: How to split screen on a Chromebook in clamshell or tablet mode
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
If you use your laptop for work, multitasking is pretty important. Split screen functionality is one of the nicest features out there to help get things done. Most people are familiar with multitasking gestures on their phone, but you can also use similar gestures or use a mouse trick to enable a split screen on your Chromebook.

In fact, there are many ways to enable split screen in ChromeOS. You can take advantage of split screen functionality in clamshell mode, tablet mode, or tent mode. In this tutorial, we'll look at all the ways you can enable split screen on your Chromebook or other ChromeOS device.

How to split screen on a Chromebook

Using your mouse

The simplest way to split screen on a Chromebook is to first open all the windows you'd like to split screen. From there onwards, there's a mouse trick that you can use in ChromeOS.

  1. Using the cursor, hover over the maximize button on the window. It's the button in the middle near the (X) for closing a window.
  2. Pick the Split option.
  3. The window you just had open will split to the left side of the screen at 50% size on your desired side of the screen.
  4. Repeat the same for the other window on the opposite side, and you’re ready to multitask.

You can long-press the maximize/minimize button next to the close window button up on the top-right of your window/app top bar and you’ll see an arrow appear on both sides. Simply slide your finger over the side you want to move the window to. This will create a 50% window on your chosen side. This is probably the fastest method for multitasking. If you love keyboard shortcuts for multitasking, check out our full guide to ChromeOS keyboard shortcuts.

Using a keyboard shortcut

Don't want to use your mouse to split screen on a Chromebook? No worries, there's a keyboard shortcut for that, too.

  1. Open the window you want to tile.
  2. Press the Alt button on your keyboard.
  3. While holding Alt, press [ to tile your window to the left.
  4. While holding Alt, press ] to tile your window to the right.

Split screen in overview mode

For a bit fancier approach, in desktop/clamshell mode, you can enter the overview mode. Overview mode is similar to the experience you get on a Windows system when you enter Task View in Windows 10 or Windows 11. Here's how you can use it.

  1. Use the shortcut key on the top of your keyboard or use a 3-finger swipe up on the trackpad to enter overview mode.
  2. Drag and drop the preview of the mini windows to either side to expand them into a split view.

No matter which method you choose in clamshell mode, you can easily resize the windows. To resize both windows simultaneously, hover the divider between the split-screen windows and a small box will appear beneath your pointer that will allow you to resize. This method shows you a real-time preview of exactly what the split screen will look like when you finish.

Split screen on a Chromebook in tablet/tent mode

If you use your Chromebook a lot as a tablet, the good news is that you can still split screen in this mode. It takes a bit more patience since you'll be using your fingers, but practice makes perfect. Note that to effectively navigate split screen in tablet or tent mode, you need to comfortably move between full-screen and overview mode. Here's how to use it.

  1. Swipe down from the top of your screen.
  2. Choose one of the formats you want to snap your window into.
  3. Tap the second open app to fill the screen.
  4. Continue this for all open apps.

In tablet mode, you can also resize your split screen apps, but you don't have full control over the sizing like in clamshell mode. To resize in tablet mode, touch the divider bar and pause for just a second until you see the bar become slightly wider. After you see this animation, drag the bar to divide the screen into a one-quarter/three-quarter layout or half-and-half. These sizing options are more limited, but they tend to work pretty well for most tablet applications on a Chromebook.

Split screen mode is easy to enable on your Chromebook. There are a number of ways to accomplish split screen view, from keyboard shortcuts to touch input. If you're still setting up a new Chromebook, don't miss our full ChromeOS setting guide for more tips.

item.title: Best bands for Apple Watch Series 9 in 2023
item.itunes:title: Best bands for Apple Watch Series 9 in 2023
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The Apple Watch Series 9 is here, arriving alongside the iPhone 15 and iPhone 15 Pro series. While it looks very similar to Apple's previous watch, it has some notable upgrades under the hood, including a faster processor and a brighter screen. Since it is the same design, all the same watch bands that worked on previous models work with the new one, which means there's a myriad of options if you want to change the look of your Apple Watch.

To help you narrow your search, we've rounded up some of our favorite options so you can choose something that looks and feels just right. Most of these come in both 41mm and 45mm sizes, but you'll have to make sure you're picking the right size for your model. We'll also note if any of them are only available in one size.

  • Apple Sport Loop
    Editor's choice
    $49 at Amazon
  • Apple FineWoven Apple Watch Band

    $99 at Apple
  • Modal Nylon (Apple Watch)
    Best value

    Leather strap
    $68 at CASETiFY
  • Hermés Toile H Band for Apple Watch
    Super premium
    $349 at Apple
  • Apple Watch Series 9
    $399 at Best Buy

item.title: ChatGPT vs Bing Chat vs Google Bard: What are the differences?
item.itunes:title: ChatGPT vs Bing Chat vs Google Bard: What are the differences?
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Quick Links

  • What is GPT?
  • ChatGPT
  • Bing Chat
  • Google Bard
  • Which should you use?

ChatGPT, Bing Chat, and Google Bard are all part of a wave of generative AI models that can take your text prompts and give you unique outputs, which can help you generate emails, process information, or research online. With all of these systems being similar, it's hard to pick which one is actually the best. So, let's analyze the differences between the big three, and which is better based on the situation.

It's important to note first that none of these AIs can think. They're merely algorithms trained to chain words and sentences together based on what they've learned. No actions are being undertaken by these generative models. They're just generating an output based on the processed input.

In that same vein, when an AI goes off the rails and starts insulting the user, it's not because it genuinely harbors resentful feelings. The internet is a vast place, and even thinking back to Tay from Microsoft years ago, training an AI on user-generated content online will eventually lead them to find negative corners of the internet and learn from them. As a result, bots know to be mean because humans can also be mean. It's not necessarily the case that Bing Chat is the second coming of Skynet.

What is GPT?

Before talking about all the various chatbots, it's important to recognize what Generative Pre-trained Transformers (GPT) are. These are a family of language models developed by OpenAI, which are trained on huge amounts of text data and are fine-tuned for natural language processing (NLP) tasks.

The most recently available version of GPT, called GPT-4, powers Bing Chat and can be accessed with ChatGPT Plus. OpenAI hasn't gone into too much public detail about it yet, but GPT-3.5, which kickstarted the AI text genereation craze and powers ChatGPT, was trained on 570GB of plaintext collected from English Wikipedia, books, and web crawling.

ChatGPT

The big one that everyone knows is ChatGPT, is arguably what got the ball rolling on AI text generation. The most recent iteration is based on ChatGPT-3.5, which is widely accessible and free for everybody on OpenAI's website, though sometimes you may need to wait for servers to free up to generate responses if you're on the free plan. ChatGPT Plus is a paid tier that gives you access to GPT-4, though as we'll get to later, you can also access GPT-4 through Bing Chat.

The biggest downside of ChatGPT was its lack of access to the wider internet, which is why Google Bard and Bing Chat were more sensible choices for someone looking to do research. However, OpenAI announced plugins that can help ChatGPT interface with the wider internet. It greatly expands its capabilities to be more competitive in this space, though there's currently a waiting list for plugin access.

From my own personal usage, ChatGPT is still pretty good, but I think it's been overtaken at this stage. However, ChatGPT has an advantage in that it's easy to use, open to everybody, great for text generation, and has an inherent first-mover advantage. It's not the best for research (as its training data doesn't include anything from the last year), but it's a solid foundation to use when you just want to get something done quickly.

Bing Chat

Bing Chat is one of the free ways you can access GPT-4 right now, and anyone can now use it. It can access the rest of the internet and will source what it finds, so that you can verify the authenticity of the information it's providing. Sure, not everyone will check the citations, but it's better than ChatGPT, which spits information at you with no way to see where it came from. You can also choose a conversation style, asking it to be more creative, more balanced, or more precise. Even better is that Microsoft has now incorporated image generation into Bing Chat, meaning you don't have to pay for Midjourney or set up a local instance of Stable Diffusion. Right now, this is one of the best ways to access the new DALL-E 3 image generation model.

Bing Chat isn't perfect, though, and there are many limitations you need to be aware of. Because of several debacles where Bing would insult its users or turn a bit... weird, Microsoft has imposed daily chat limits. There are limits on the total number of queries in a day and the total number of messages in a conversation, which limits the ability for Bing Chat to go off the rails. The limit is also presumably for some cost-saving reasons on Microsoft's end. Microsoft has also expanded Bing Chat to work on other browsers like Chrome and Safari, though Firefox and other browser users will need to modify their browser's user agent to use it.

The strengths of Bing Chat are its slightly more advanced language model, and you can also choose a conversation "style" to be more precise, more creative, or a balance of the two. However, it falls a bit behind because of its limitations. Bing Chat is, most of all, an extension to its search engine, so it's best used for research. This differs wildly from Google's approach.

Google Bard

Google Bard has seen a massive glow-up since its launch. It's no longer limited to just a handful of regions, and can be access throughout most of the world, including the European Union. Not only has it improved its availability, it's also just gotten better in general.

The difference with Google Bard versus its competitors is that Google is using entirely different technology to power its chatbot, as the initial technology used is based on the company's Transformer that it first unveiled in 2017. Google's in-house LaMDA built on Transformer, and Bard became an extension of LaMDA that the company teased a couple of years ago at Google I/O. From what some users saw at the start, it seemed to be a bit behind what the others are doing currently. The company definitely experienced some growing pains when it came to misinformation and fact-checking, but the situation has massively improved.

Google was surprisingly on the back foot, but it clearly didn't want to stay that way. Bard was advertised as a "complement" to Search but is now being integrated into the Google Assistant on select smartphones. The company clearly didn't feel confident in Bard at first, but that seems to be changing. We've seen Bard improve leaps and bounds over the last few months, with the company announcing PaLM 2 as its next-generation language model to power much of its AI work.

Which should you use?

If you're going to use any generative AI model, then Google Bard is honestly, for my money, the best around. Once upon a time it would have been ChatGPT or Bing Chat, but Bing Chat's lock-in to, well, Bing, puts me off. Also, Bard doesn't have a turn limit.

What's surprising is that Google Bard was once too far behind to even be considered an alternative right now, but the reason Bard gets a win from me is when it comes to coding. While it's great at research, writing, and proofreading, it's also been a massive help when it comes to Python.

For what AI is currently safest, ChatGPT probably takes the crown. Researching new topics with AI tools like Bard and Bing Chat is a recipe for disaster, as we've already seen, whereas ChatGPT is great for helping you write emails or come up with ideas. Bing Chat and Bard both have the benefit of at least showing you sources, but the limited search volume of Bing Chat is a big negative against it, and Bard can still simply pull up wrong sources.

Right now, this is one of the best ways to access the new DALL-E 3 image generation model.

Bing Chat isn't perfect, though, and there are many limitations you need to be aware of. Because of several debacles where Bing would insult its users or turn a bit... weird, Microsoft has imposed daily chat limits. There are limits on the total number of queries in a day and the total number of messages in a conversation, which limits the ability for Bing Chat to go off the rails. The limit is also presumably for some cost-saving reasons on Microsoft's end. Microsoft has also expanded Bing Chat to work on other browsers like Chrome and Safari, though Firefox and other browser users will need to modify their browser's user agent to use it.

The strengths of Bing Chat are its slightly more advanced language model, and you can also choose a conversation "style" to be more precise, more creative, or a balance of the two. However, it falls a bit behind because of its limitations. Bing Chat is, most of all, an extension to its search engine, so it's best used for research. This differs wildly from Google's approach.

Google Bard

Google Bard has seen a massive glow-up since its launch. It's no longer limited to just a handful of regions, and can be access throughout most of the world, including the European Union. Not only has it improved its availability, it's also just gotten better in general.

The difference with Google Bard versus its competitors is that Google is using entirely different technology to power its chatbot, as the initial technology used is based on the company's Transformer that it first unveiled in 2017. Google's in-house LaMDA built on Transformer, and Bard became an extension of LaMDA that the company teased a couple of years ago at Google I/O. From what some users saw at the start, it seemed to be a bit behind what the others are doing currently. The company definitely experienced some growing pains when it came to misinformation and fact-checking, but the situation has massively improved.

Google was surprisingly on the back foot, but it clearly didn't want to stay that way. Bard was advertised as a "complement" to Search but is now being integrated into the Google Assistant on select smartphones. The company clearly didn't feel confident in Bard at first, but that seems to be changing. We've seen Bard improve leaps and bounds over the last few months, with the company announcing PaLM 2 as its next-generation language model to power much of its AI work.

Which should you use?

If you're going to use any generative AI model, then Google Bard is honestly, for my money, the best around. Once upon a time it would have been ChatGPT or Bing Chat, but Bing Chat's lock-in to, well, Bing, puts me off. Also, Bard doesn't have a turn limit.

What's surprising is that Google Bard was once too far behind to even be considered an alternative right now, but the reason Bard gets a win from me is when it comes to coding. While it's great at research, writing, and proofreading, it's also been a massive help when it comes to Python.

For what AI is currently safest, ChatGPT probably takes the crown. Researching new topics with AI tools like Bard and Bing Chat is a recipe for disaster, as we've already seen, whereas ChatGPT is great for helping you write emails or come up with ideas. Bing Chat and Bard both have the benefit of at least showing you sources, but the limited search volume of Bing Chat is a big negative against it, and Bard can still simply pull up wrong sources.
item.content:encoded:
Quick Links

  • What is GPT?
  • ChatGPT
  • Bing Chat
  • Google Bard
  • Which should you use?

ChatGPT, Bing Chat, and Google Bard are all part of a wave of generative AI models that can take your text prompts and give you unique outputs, which can help you generate emails, process information, or research online. With all of these systems being similar, it's hard to pick which one is actually the best. So, let's analyze the differences between the big three, and which is better based on the situation.

It's important to note first that none of these AIs can think. They're merely algorithms trained to chain words and sentences together based on what they've learned. No actions are being undertaken by these generative models. They're just generating an output based on the processed input.

In that same vein, when an AI goes off the rails and starts insulting the user, it's not because it genuinely harbors resentful feelings. The internet is a vast place, and even thinking back to Tay from Microsoft years ago, training an AI on user-generated content online will eventually lead them to find negative corners of the internet and learn from them. As a result, bots know to be mean because humans can also be mean. It's not necessarily the case that Bing Chat is the second coming of Skynet.

What is GPT?

Before talking about all the various chatbots, it's important to recognize what Generative Pre-trained Transformers (GPT) are. These are a family of language models developed by OpenAI, which are trained on huge amounts of text data and are fine-tuned for natural language processing (NLP) tasks.

The most recently available version of GPT, called GPT-4, powers Bing Chat and can be accessed with ChatGPT Plus. OpenAI hasn't gone into too much public detail about it yet, but GPT-3.5, which kickstarted the AI text genereation craze and powers ChatGPT, was trained on 570GB of plaintext collected from English Wikipedia, books, and web crawling.

ChatGPT

The big one that everyone knows is ChatGPT, is arguably what got the ball rolling on AI text generation. The most recent iteration is based on ChatGPT-3.5, which is widely accessible and free for everybody on OpenAI's website, though sometimes you may need to wait for servers to free up to generate responses if you're on the free plan. ChatGPT Plus is a paid tier that gives you access to GPT-4, though as we'll get to later, you can also access GPT-4 through Bing Chat.

The biggest downside of ChatGPT was its lack of access to the wider internet, which is why Google Bard and Bing Chat were more sensible choices for someone looking to do research. However, OpenAI announced plugins that can help ChatGPT interface with the wider internet. It greatly expands its capabilities to be more competitive in this space, though there's currently a waiting list for plugin access.

From my own personal usage, ChatGPT is still pretty good, but I think it's been overtaken at this stage. However, ChatGPT has an advantage in that it's easy to use, open to everybody, great for text generation, and has an inherent first-mover advantage. It's not the best for research (as its training data doesn't include anything from the last year), but it's a solid foundation to use when you just want to get something done quickly.

Bing Chat

Bing Chat is one of the free ways you can access GPT-4 right now, and anyone can now use it. It can access the rest of the internet and will source what it finds, so that you can verify the authenticity of the information it's providing. Sure, not everyone will check the citations, but it's better than ChatGPT, which spits information at you with no way to see where it came from. You can also choose a conversation style, asking it to be more creative, more balanced, or more precise. Even better is that Microsoft has now incorporated image generation into Bing Chat, meaning you don't have to pay for Midjourney or set up a local instance of Stable Diffusion. Right now, this is one of the best ways to access the new DALL-E 3 image generation model.

Bing Chat isn't perfect, though, and there are many limitations you need to be aware of. Because of several debacles where Bing would insult its users or turn a bit... weird, Microsoft has imposed daily chat limits. There are limits on the total number of queries in a day and the total number of messages in a conversation, which limits the ability for Bing Chat to go off the rails. The limit is also presumably for some cost-saving reasons on Microsoft's end. Microsoft has also expanded Bing Chat to work on other browsers like Chrome and Safari, though Firefox and other browser users will need to modify their browser's user agent to use it.

The strengths of Bing Chat are its slightly more advanced language model, and you can also choose a conversation "style" to be more precise, more creative, or a balance of the two. However, it falls a bit behind because of its limitations. Bing Chat is, most of all, an extension to its search engine, so it's best used for research. This differs wildly from Google's approach.

Google Bard

Google Bard has seen a massive glow-up since its launch. It's no longer limited to just a handful of regions, and can be access throughout most of the world, including the European Union. Not only has it improved its availability, it's also just gotten better in general.

The difference with Google Bard versus its competitors is that Google is using entirely different technology to power its chatbot, as the initial technology used is based on the company's Transformer that it first unveiled in 2017. Google's in-house LaMDA built on Transformer, and Bard became an extension of LaMDA that the company teased a couple of years ago at Google I/O. From what some users saw at the start, it seemed to be a bit behind what the others are doing currently. The company definitely experienced some growing pains when it came to misinformation and fact-checking, but the situation has massively improved.

Google was surprisingly on the back foot, but it clearly didn't want to stay that way. Bard was advertised as a "complement" to Search but is now being integrated into the Google Assistant on select smartphones. The company clearly didn't feel confident in Bard at first, but that seems to be changing. We've seen Bard improve leaps and bounds over the last few months, with the company announcing PaLM 2 as its next-generation language model to power much of its AI work.

Which should you use?

If you're going to use any generative AI model, then Google Bard is honestly, for my money, the best around. Once upon a time it would have been ChatGPT or Bing Chat, but Bing Chat's lock-in to, well, Bing, puts me off. Also, Bard doesn't have a turn limit.

What's surprising is that Google Bard was once too far behind to even be considered an alternative right now, but the reason Bard gets a win from me is when it comes to coding. While it's great at research, writing, and proofreading, it's also been a massive help when it comes to Python.

For what AI is currently safest, ChatGPT probably takes the crown. Researching new topics with AI tools like Bard and Bing Chat is a recipe for disaster, as we've already seen, whereas ChatGPT is great for helping you write emails or come up with ideas. Bing Chat and Bard both have the benefit of at least showing you sources, but the limited search volume of Bing Chat is a big negative against it, and Bard can still simply pull up wrong sources.

item.title: Best chargers for Apple Watch Series 9 in 2023

item.itunes:summary:
So, you've got yourself an Apple Watch Series 9, which happens to be one of the best smartwatches, and you've already picked out an Apple Watch Series 9 case, so now what? Well, another important accessory to think about is a charger. Making sure you've got a good charger that fits your lifestyle and budget is a good idea, but it's also a good idea to make sure you've got a backup charger, in addition to your standard, official charger, just in case something happens.

All of the above adds up to needing to find a good charger for your Apple Watch. To that end, we've put together the ultimate list of Apple Watch Series 9 chargers, so regardless of who you are, you're sure to find a charger below that will work for you.

item.title: Best T-Mobile phones in 2023
item.itunes:title: Best T-Mobile phones in 2023
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
T-Mobile is one of the biggest wireless carriers in the U.S., with a variety of smartphones and plans available. Whether you're looking for a budget-friendly phone, a high-end flagship, or something in between, T-Mobile has you covered. Almost all smartphones that make it to our collection of the best smartphones are readily available to purchase via T-Mobile in the U.S., making it one of the best carriers around. 2023 has been a pretty good year for smartphones and we've seen some big smartphone releases over the past few months, so let's turn the spotlight on some of the phones you can buy from T-Mobile in 2023.

  • Source: Samsung 
    Samsung Galaxy S23 Ultra
    Best overall
    $1170 at T-Mobile (via Samsung)
  • Source: Apple
    Apple iPhone 15 Pro Max
    Best iPhone
    $1200 at T-Mobile
  • Source: Google
    Google Pixel 8 Pro
    Best Android experience
    $1000 at T-Mobile
  • Google Pixel 7a
    Best value for money pick
    Up to $500 off with trade-in
  • Source: Samsung 
    Samsung Galaxy A54 5G
    Best mid-range phone
    $99 at T-Mobile
  • Source: Motorola
    Moto G Stylus 5G (2023)
    For stylus lovers
    $300 at T-Mobile
  • OnePlus Nord N30 5G
    Best under $300
    $264 at T-Mobile
  • Moto Razr+ (2023)
    Best clamshell foldable
    $1000 at T-Mobile
  • Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 5
    Best big-sized foldable phone
    $1800 at T-Mobile

Best T-Mobile phones in 2023

Source: Samsung 
Samsung Galaxy S23 Ultra
Best overall

The most powerful flagship on the market

$1170 $1200 Save $30

The Galaxy S23 Ultra is one of the best phones on the market, packing an all-new 200MP sensor, a refined design, a custom Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 for Galaxy chipset, and One UI 5.1.

Pros
  • Smooth, fast performance
  • Great battery life
  • Superb cameras
  • Top-notch display
Cons
  • New camera features aren't easy to find
  • Expensive
  • Speakers are hit and miss
  • Charging speeds aren't up to par with the price
$1170 at T-Mobile (via Samsung) $1200 at T-Mobile

The Galaxy S23 Ultra is the best phone in Samsung's latest batch of 2023 flagships, differentiating itself with a powerful set of internals, a 200MP camera, an S Pen stylus, and more. It also packs a massive battery and the biggest display of all three phones for a more immersive user experience.

One of the best things about the Galaxy S23 Ultra is that it comes with a custom Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 chip. This is one of the most powerful chips on the market right now, and it works very well with One UI software to deliver a fluid experience. Also new to Galaxy flagships this year is UFS 4.0 storage, which is better than the UFS 3.1 storage we got in the previous-gen flagship.

Samsung has also packed in a 200MP camera on the Galaxy S23 Ultra that uses the new Adapt Pixel sensor to bring many improvements to the photography experience. The device will automatically decide whether to take a full 200MP shot or bin it down into a 50 or 12MP image. As mentioned in our Galaxy S23 Ultra review, leaving it in the automatic mode can fetch you some good results, so I recommend doing that unless you want to fiddle with different settings. The main 200MP camera is also complemented by two 10MP sensors with a 10x and 3x optical zoom and a 12MP ultrawide sensor.

There are a ton of other things to like about the Galaxy S23 Ultra, so be sure to check out our review before making a purchase decision. T-Mobile is currently offering an $800 discount when you purchase the phone with a new line on Go5G Plus or when you trade in an eligible device on a qualifying rate plan. They're both great ways to save some money on your purchase, so be sure to check them out.

Source: Apple
Apple iPhone 15 Pro Max
Best iPhone

The best iPhone money can buy this year

The iPhone 15 Pro Max introduces an all-new, customizable Action button, a titanium chassis with round edges, and a USB Type-C port. It exclusively offers up to 5x optical zoom, too.

Pros
  • Much improved in-hand feel compared to previous Pro Max iPhones
  • Powerful A17 Pro chip
  • More focal versatility including longer zoom
Cons
  • Slow charging speeds
  • Big footprint
$1200 at T-Mobile

Unlike the last few iPhone Pro Max models, the 6.7-inch iPhone 15 Pro Max isn't just a larger iPhone 15 Pro this year. Apple has taken active measures to ensure it packs a better hardware overall to make it stand out in the iPhone 15 line up. That's right, the iPhone 15 Pro Max model comes with some exclusive features, making it the best iPhone Apple has to offer in 2023. This, however, also means you need to put up with a massive footprint of the iPhone 15 Pro Max to get the best iPhone features, so keep that in mind.

One of the first differences between the iPhone 15 Pro and the iPhone 15 Pro Max is that the bigger iPhone comes with a larger display with support for higher resolution to ensure it doesn't lose the sharpness due to the bigger size. The iPhone 15 Pro model also features a new tetraprism lens with support for up to 5x optical zoom, making it better than the regular iPhone 15 Pro. It also packs a bigger battery overall, meaning it can last longer between the charges.

The rest of the features are all the same, these neat additions make the iPhone 15 Pro Max a better pick this year. I suggest stopping by iPhone 15 Pro vs iPhone 15 Pro Max comparison to find out which one is better for you in 2023. Those buying the iPhone 15 Pro Max from T-Mobile can save up to $830 on their purchase provided they add a new line on a qualifying plan and bring an eligible phone for a trade in.

Source: Google
Google Pixel 8 Pro
Best Android experience

For Android purists

The Pixel 8 Pro is the latest flagship from Google, and it packs the best the company has to offer in 2023. It features the newest Tensor G3 processor, like its regular Pixel 8 sibling, but it comes with a 6.7-inch OLED display, a brighter screen, a larger battery, and more storage options.

Pros
  • Good design and build quality
  • Impressive cameras with AI smarts
  • Reliable performance for day-to-day usage
Cons
  • Not a significant upgrade over the Pixel 7 Pro
  • Costs more than its predecessor
$1000 at T-Mobile

Google's new Pixel 8 Pro may not seem like a radical improvement over the outgoing mode, it brings a few noteworthy changes to build upon its predecessor. Google has packed this particular handset with its new Tensor G3 chip, which is the first silicon to support company's on-device machine learning algorithms to enable some amazing features. All Pixel 8 Pro models also come with 12GB RAM out of the box as opposed to just 8GB.

The Pixel 8 Pro, as you'd expect, also comes with a better set of cameras. It packs a 50MP main camera along with dual 48MP sensors for telephoto and ultrawide shots. They all work together to deliver some great results. Google's on-device machine learning algorithm also enable some jaw-dropping camera/editing features on the Pixel 8 Pro. I suggest reading our Pixel 8 Pro review to learn more about these features in detail.

The Pixel 8 Pro also packs a slightly bigger battery with support for faster charging, and it also sports a new temperature sensor at the back. Those who are looking to buy this particular handset from T-Mobile can save as much as $800 on their purchase via 24 monthly bill credits by adding a new line on a Go5G Next or Go5G Plus plan. You can also stop by our Pixel 8 Pro vs Pixel 7 Pro to find out which flagship phone is better for you.

Google Pixel 7a
Best value for money pick

Brings features that are hard to find for $500

Google's latest mid-ranger brings several improvements over last year's model, making it a great option for the budget conscious. The Pixel 7a packs the flagship Tensor G2 chip, flagship-tier cameras, an improved 90Hz display, more RAM, better durability, and wireless charging support.

Pros
  • Overall performance very close to the Pixel 7
  • The best camera for a mid-ranger
  • Looks and feels like a flagship phone in hand
Cons
  • Really, really slow charging
Up to $500 off with trade-in

Google's Pixel 7a is just a no-brainer when it comes to the best budget phones that cost less than $500. This new phone from Google brings impressive hardware along with a solid set of features that are simply hard to find in its price range. It's a bit more expensive than its closest competitor, the Galaxy A54 5G, but things like a flagship chipset, great cameras, and support for wireless charging make it an excellent phone for $500.

The Pixel 7a, in case you're wondering, sports a 6.1-inch display with support for up to 90Hz refresh rate. A low refresh rate panel was one of the biggest complaints we had with the Pixel 6a last year, so we're glad to see that changing with its successor. Oh yeah, it's also getting wireless charging, a first for Google's A-series of phones. The new phone also comes with Google's new Tensor G2 chip, which is the same processor that's running the show inside the flagship Pixel 7 and Pixel 7 Pro.

Google has fitted the Pixel 7a with a new set of camera sensors. It gets a 64MP main sensor along with a 13MP ultrawide camera, both of which manage to produce some good results. You can check out some camera samples along with a lot more details about this phone in our Pixel 7a review. Alternatively, you can also consider checking out the Galaxy S23 FE that costs slightly more, but brings better internals and a more beautiful AMOLED panel. Our Galaxy S23 FE vs Pixel 7a has more details in case you're wondering which one is better for you, so be sure to check it out.

Source: Samsung 
Samsung Galaxy A54 5G
Best mid-range phone

A budget phone with a flagship-grade display

$99 $450 Save $351

The Samsung Galaxy A54 5G is a great mid-range phone that offers a sleek design and a beautiful 120Hz display. It's easily one of the best phones you can pick up from T-Mobile right now.

Pros
  • Beautiful design
  • Reliable day-to-day performance
  • Long-lasting battery
Cons
  • Macro sensor is average at best
  • Slow charging speeds
$99 at T-Mobile

Samsung's Galaxy A54 5G is currently among the best budget phones you can buy in the U.S., and it's readily available to purchase from T-Mobile as well. It brings a lot of improvements over the outgoing Galaxy A53 model, and you can rely on it to deliver a good day-to-day performance.

One of my favorite features of this particular handset is its 6.5-inch 120Hz OLED panel, which makes it better than pretty much every other phone in its segment. The colors look excellent on OLED, and it features Samsung's enhanced Vision Booster technology to adjust your display in outdoor settings. It's just perfect for all forms of media consumption, be it watching videos or playing games. The 120Hz panel truly makes it a superior option in the budget space.

The Galaxy A54 5G is also packed with an upgraded Exynos 1380 chipset, offering a boost in performance compared to other competing chips in its class. It also comes with 6GB of RAM and 128GB of storage, so your apps will run smoothly. There's even expandable storage of up to 1TB via a microSD card. Some other highlights of the phone include 5G support, an IP67 rating for dust and water resistance, a triple camera setup at the back that takes good pictures (unless you want to use the macro lens), great battery life that'll last a full day, and more.

T-Mobile will let you have a Galaxy A54 5G unit for as little as $99, provided you activate a new line and sign up for 24-monthly bills.

Source: Motorola
Moto G Stylus 5G (2023)
For stylus lovers

Get it for FREE with a new line

This stylus-equipped Moto G has a long battery life, 5G connectivity, and a Snapdragon 600 series CPU.

Pros
  • Great battery life
  • Solid Android software with Motorola's slight touch
  • A great screen with stylus support
Cons
  • Camera suffers in low-light conditions
  • The display could've been brighter
$300 at T-Mobile

The Moto G Stylus 5G remains a solid option for those who are looking to buy a budget phone with a stylus. The 2023 model is packed with some powerful internals, and it's easily among the best Motorola phones you can buy right now. The highlight of the Moto G Stylus 5G, as you'd expect, is the stylus itself. You can use it for taking notes, doodling, or simply navigating through the phone's UI. Motorola could've done a better job of optimizing the software to take better advantage of the stylus, but you do get all the basics.

The 6.6-inch display with an FHD+ 2400x1080 resolution is also quite nice to use with the stylus, although it could've been brighter. It is, however, a smooth and responsive panel with support for 120Hz refresh rate, though, so no complaints there. The phone also offers reliable performance for day-to-day usage, and you'll also be able to capture some good photos using its cameras, provided there's enough light in the scene.

Motorola is also selling a cheaper version of this phone for $200, which is also called the Moto G Stylus (without the 5G), but it skips a lot of essentials, including 5G, a high-resolution display, NFC, and more. Alternatively, you can also consider buying the TCL Stylus 5G from T-Mobile. It's not as capable as the new Moto G Stylus 5G, but it still offers a good experience with a reliable passive stylus.

OnePlus Nord N30 5G
Best under $300

Best for those who only have $300 to spare

The OnePlus Nord N30 5G brings the expected fast and smooth experience of a OnePlus phone along with an impressive 108MP camera at a great price. You can also get one for free from T-Mobile when you add a new line.

Pros
  • Beautiful design
  • Fast UI and capable processor
  • Affordable price tag
Cons
  • The build quality could've been better
  • Underwhelming camera performance
$264 at T-Mobile

The OnePlus Nord N30 5G isn't a significant upgrade over the outgoing Nord N20 model, but it's still worth considering in 2023. One thing I really like about this particular phone is its design. The Nord N30 5G is easily among the best-looking phones in its segment. It is, however, made out of plastic, so it may not feel as premium as it looks in hand. That's not necessarily a deal-breaker, especially if you plan on using a case to protect it.

Another highlight of this phone is its 5,000mAh battery with support for up to 55W wired charging. The OnePlus Nord N30 5G is the only phone in its price range to support "true" fast charging. OnePlus also bundles the phone with a compatible fast charger, meaning you don't have to spend any more money on the phone to get the best experience.

Some other noteworthy features of the OnePlus Nord N30 5G include a 6.7-inch display with a 120Hz refresh rate, a 3.5m headphone jack, a microSD card slot for storage expansion, and more. I suggest you read our OnePlus Nord N30 5G review to learn more about this before picking it up. T-Mobile will let you have this phone for free, provided you add a new line and sign up to pay 24 monthly bills. It's also a bit cheaper at T-Mobile compared to other retailers, even if you choose to pay in full, so it's definitely worth considering.\

Moto Razr+ (2023)
Best clamshell foldable

Finally a good competition to Samsung's Galaxy Z Flip 4

The Moto Razr+ is a slightly more premium version of the regular Moto Razr, with a relatively bigger external display, a Snapdragon 8+ Gen 1 chip, and more. T-Mobile will let you have this clamshell for free if you get a new line on Go5G Next or Go5G Plus.

Pros
  • Large outside screen lets you do a lot without unfolding
  • Bright, vivid main folding display
  • Sleek and compact form factor
Cons
  • The battery life could've been better
$1000 at T-Mobile

Motorola's Razr+ (2023) is a modern take on its classic Razr phone that comes with a folding screen. The 2023 model is significantly better than the last couple of iterations we saw from the company in the foldable space. What makes the new Moto Razr+ better than the other clamshell phone is its massive outer display that now lets you do a lot without unfolding the phone. Samsung's new Galaxy Z Flip 5 is equally good as it also sports a larger cover screen now, but there are a few things which the Razr+ does slightly better in comparison. For instance, there's no noticeable crease on the internal display of the Razr+, and it also lets you run pretty much any application on its cover screen without need for any workarounds.

You are looking at a 3.6-inch 144Hz OLED panel on the outside which is about as big as it can physically get. It even wraps around the dual-camera system, meaning it occupies the entire front half of the device. Motorola doesn't limit what you can do on the outer screen, so you can use to open and use any app you want. This eliminates the need to unfold the phone and access the main folding screen more often throughout the day.

The main display on the inside is also a solid 6.9-inch OLED panel with support for up to 165Hz refresh rate. The phone is powered by Snapdragon 8+ Gen 1 chipset, and it also comes with a 3,800mAh battery with support for up to 30W wired and 5W wireless charging. T-Mobile is carrying the phone in both "Infinite Black" and "Viva Magenta" colors, and it'll let you have one for free, provided you add a new line on Go5G Plus or Magenta MAX or bring an eligible phone for a trade-in.

Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 5
Best big-sized foldable phone

Best foldable in the U.S. for power users

Samsung's latest Galaxy Z Fold 5 is a further refinement to the Z Fold lineup. The smartphone looks similar to past Z Fold iterations, featuring a 7.6-inch main screen and a tall cover screen. T-Mobile is currently offering a $1000 discount on this phone for those who get a new line on Go5G Next or Go5G Plus.

Pros
  • New hinge that now folds flat
  • Relatively light at 253g
  • Still the best hinge in the business
Cons
  • Other foldables are thinner with bigger batteries
  • No camera improvements compared to last year
  • Design starting to look very dated
$1800 at T-Mobile

Samsung's new Galaxy Z Fold 5 isn't a significant upgrade over its predecessor, the Galaxy Z Fold 4, but it packs a lot of improvements to deliver a more refined user experience that will make it one of the best foldable phones of the year. The new foldable comes with a redesigned hinge made of fewer parts and folds the screen at a softer waterdrop angle. It allows the Galaxy Z Fold 5 to fold completely flat with no gaps in the middle, but the display still has a visible crease. The newer model is also slightly thinner and lighter than its predecessor.

The Galaxy Z Fold 5 has the same displays as its predecessor, meaning you get a 6.2-inch AMOLED cover screen and a 7.6-inch AMOLED main screen, both of which have support for up to 120Hz refresh rate. Both panels also have support for S Pen, but you'll have to buy it separately as it's still not included with the device.

Samsung has, however, changed the internals on the updated model. The Galaxy Z Fold 5 comes with Qualcomm's Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 for Galaxy chip, which is the same as the Galaxy S23 models. It's among the best mobile chips out there right now, and it's paired with 12GB of RAM for the best performance. Samsung hasn't improved the cameras this year, so you get the same setup as the last year's model. I suggest you stop by my colleague Ben's Galaxy Z Fold 5 review to check out the camera samples and learn more about the device.

Best T-Mobile phones: The bottom line

Samsung's Galaxy S23 Ultra is the one I recommend if you're looking to pick up a solid phone from T-Mobile right now. It packs powerful internals and comes with a good set of features to deliver a great experience overall. It's powered by the new Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 for Galaxy chip, making it one of the most powerful phones you can buy right now. Pair that with 12GB of RAM and UFS 4.0 storage, and you have a device that'll serve you well for many years to come. The new 200MP camera also plays a huge role in delivering some solid photos, and the built-in S Pen just makes it a versatile device to use on a daily basis.

Those who are looking to buy an iPhone can consider looking at the iPhone 15 Pro Max model, as it represents the best of what Apple has to offer in 2023. The iPhone 15 Pro and the regular iPhone 15 are also great options to consider in case you don't want to use a massive phone or are looking to buy something cheaper. Samsung's Galaxy Z Fold 5 and the Moto Razr+ are also good options to consider if you're looking to get into foldable phones this year. Lastly, I've also highlighted some affordable phones for those who are working with a relatively tight budget, so be sure to explore all the options before making a purchase decision.

There's no shortage of phones that work with T-Mobile in the U.S., but these are the only ones that are worth considering in 2023. This is an ever-evolving list that I'll update frequently to add more relevant options, so be sure to check back for newer and potentially better phones. There's a lot more in store for us in terms of smartphone launches in 2023, and I expect the list to expand very soon with relatively newer phones.

Source: Samsung 
Samsung Galaxy S23 Ultra
Best overall
$1170 $1200 Save $30

The Galaxy S23 Ultra is one of the best phones on the market, packing an all-new 200MP sensor, a refined design, a custom Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 for Galaxy chipset, and One UI 5.1.

$1170 at T-Mobile (via Samsung)

Whether you're looking for a budget-friendly phone, a high-end flagship, or something in between, T-Mobile has you covered. Almost all smartphones that make it to our collection of the best smartphones are readily available to purchase via T-Mobile in the U.S., making it one of the best carriers around. 2023 has been a pretty good year for smartphones and we've seen some big smartphone releases over the past few months, so let's turn the spotlight on some of the phones you can buy from T-Mobile in 2023.

  • Source: Samsung
